title: How are the Evas not sinking into the ground
display_title: How are the Evas not sinking into the ground
wikitext:
[]: How are the Evas not sinking into the ground?             Quote Post by SuperTechmarine » Sat Sep 27, 2014 2:58 am Even the most conservative estimates put the Evangelions at over 700 tons. Their boots aren't exactly going to help them with their narrow surfaces. So that's the question. How are they not destroying the roads they walk on? The earth they tread on? Is it some AT Field trickery that's holding them up? Or is Gainax just ignorant in the way of Physics?

[]:             Quote Post by pwhodges » Sat Sep 27, 2014 3:23 am It's sometimes called "rubber physics". Your example is only one of many ways in which the Eva violate the laws of physics (as do all large mechas as normally depicted - and indeed pretty much every science-fiction story ever written). Just accept it; that's not what the show's about.

[]: Re: How are the Evas not sinking into the ground?             Quote Post by Jäeger » Sat Sep 27, 2014 3:36 am SuperTechmarine wrote: Even the most conservative estimates put the Evangelions at over 700 tons. Their boots aren't exactly going to help them with their narrow surfaces. So that's the question. How are they not destroying the roads they walk on? The earth they tread on? Is it some AT Field trickery that's holding them up? Or is Gainax just ignorant in the way of Physics?

[]:             Quote Post by Squigsquasher » Sat Sep 27, 2014 7:51 am Aside of course from the most obvious answer (anime magic) the Evangelion's A.T. Field probably plays a large role in it. I imagine the field somehow negates their weight or repels the ground such that they don't immediately sink into the ground. I think it's mentioned in the CI that both Angels and Evas are technically physically impossible, and can only exist thanks to their immensely strong A.T. Fields, which stop them collapsing under their own weight. If the A.T. Field can let Angels levitate I see no reason why it couldn't be used to stop them sinking into the earth.

[]:             Quote Post by FreakyFilmFan4ever » Sat Sep 27, 2014 8:35 am Anything that defies physics should always be explained with AT Fields, even if the action being explained has nothing to do with how or why AT Fields operate. It’s Eva Lore Law.

[]:             Quote Post by Jäeger » Sat Sep 27, 2014 8:39 am Seriously, 40 years after Mazinger Z...people have doubts like this????

[]:             Quote Post by Kendrix » Sat Sep 27, 2014 11:12 am They ARE shown to break a lot of stuff wherever they step (see the run to catch Sahaquiel, or Asuka's various attempts at battle acrobatics), so they are intended to actually have immense mass, but maybe the authors never did the math for how heavy they'd actually be. ATF magic is the likeliest candidate for an in-universe explanation, tho, especially since "why don't they collapse under their own weight" (much more directly related to 'structural integrity') would be a concern long before "sinking into the ground".

[]:             Quote Post by NemZ » Sat Sep 27, 2014 2:39 pm Don't neglect the other source of vague plot magic, "particle-wave matter.". Or hell, maybe they just built Tokyo 3 really densely to compensate.

[]:             Quote Post by TMBounty_Hunter » Sat Sep 27, 2014 2:58 pm FreakyFilmFan4ever wrote: Anything that defies physics should always be explained with AT Fields, even if the action being explained has nothing to do with how or why AT Fields operate.

[]:             Quote Post by Nuclear Lunchbox » Sat Sep 27, 2014 3:15 pm Or, as stated earlier, rubbery physics.

[]:             Quote Post by K40s » Sat Sep 27, 2014 7:58 pm I don't see the problem here... crushing street pavement, most likely yes, I even think there are scenes that depict just that... but collapsing the soil under their mass? collapsing themselves? Some dinosaurs are good examples of living organisms that didn't collapse themselves on their own mass neither collapse the soil around them...

[]:             Quote Post by ChaddyManPrime » Sat Sep 27, 2014 8:20 pm ^ Yes but even the heaviest only weighed 100 tonnes. The Evas should be collapsing under its own weight... unless the restraining armor also acts as a stabilizer. It could also explain why the Evas get on all fours when it is removed. Also it's shown that the AT field can negate gravity so perhaps the weight is reduced?

[]:             Quote Post by Sgt. Griff » Sat Sep 27, 2014 8:45 pm It's like Godzilla, in that I think we should all just overlook this small bit of "heavy boots" physics, and just watch the show. They would collapse under their weight, and then fall through the ground into the geofront due to the violent impulse on the ground. But if we're discussing actual physics here, how can AT fields exist, and floating polyhedron aliens which scream. It's science fiction dudes, doesn't need to make sense.

[]:             Quote Post by Monk Ed » Sat Sep 27, 2014 10:09 pm Before internet nerds (I use that term lovingly) told me I should care about the physics in my science fiction, I didn't. The same way I never stood up during a Looney Tunes show as a kid and scream "BULLSHIT! Rabbits don't talk!"

[]:             Quote Post by Reichu » Sun Sep 28, 2014 12:08 am K40s wrote: Some dinosaurs are good examples of living organisms that didn't collapse themselves on their own mass neither collapse the soil around them...

[]:             Quote Post by Ultrahuntr » Mon Sep 29, 2014 3:25 pm Squigsquasher wrote: Aside of course from the most obvious answer (anime magic) the Evangelion's A.T. Field probably plays a large role in it. I imagine the field somehow negates their weight or repels the ground such that they don't immediately sink into the ground.

[]:             Quote Post by FreakyFilmFan4ever » Mon Sep 29, 2014 3:52 pm Reichu wrote: Sauropods gradually evolved to be capable of toting around so much mass, whereas Evas are basically scaled-up humans who somehow have the agility of gymnasts.

[]:             Quote Post by Reichu » Mon Sep 29, 2014 4:08 pm FreakyFilmFan4ever wrote: But I generally stay closer to the side of "rubber physics" than I do AT Fields. My understanding of AT Fields was that they seem to have more to do with the emotional state of the Eva pilot and how it results in shielding them in battle. Making them just snowshoes seems to cheapen the physical manifestation of the pilot’s emotions.

[]:             Quote Post by pwhodges » Mon Sep 29, 2014 6:34 pm As far as I can see, using AT fields for this is pretty much rubber physics anyway.

[]:             Quote Post by Ultrahuntr » Tue Sep 30, 2014 4:03 pm Just saying "rubber physics" and actually attempting to give an explanation as to what you mean by that are two different things though. I (and others) were trying to give an actual explanation, not just two words.
